name: Arithmetic Progressions — Numbers in Steady Steps
whatIsIt: An Arithmetic Progression (AP) is a list of numbers where each term jumps by the SAME amount every time. That fixed jump is the common difference d. The first term is a. Any term is a_n = a + (n − 1)d, and the sum of the first n terms is S_n = n/2 × [2a + (n − 1)d], which is also n/2 × (first + last).
whyNeeded: APs describe anything that grows or shrinks by equal steps: saving a fixed amount each month, seats increasing row by row in a theatre, a salary that rises by a fixed raise, or stacked pipes. Instead of adding term by term, one formula jumps straight to the 100th term or the total.
realLifeUses: Monthly savings that grow by a fixed amount
Seats per row in an auditorium (rows increase by a fixed number)
A salary rising by the same increment each year
Total bricks in a stack that narrows by one each layer

vocabulary.meaning: The fixed amount added to get from one term to the next: d = a_2 − a_1.
The starting number of the AP.
The term in position n, found by a_n = a + (n − 1)d.
The total of the first n terms: S_n = n/2 × (2a + (n − 1)d).

story.title: Meera's Piggy Bank Plan
story.text: Meera puts 100 rupees in her piggy bank in the first month, then 20 rupees more each month than the month before: 100, 120, 140, 160… 'How much will I add in the 12th month?' she asks. Amma smiles: 'Same jump every time — that's an AP. The 12th month is 100 + 11 × 20 = 320 rupees.' Meera also wants the total saved in a year, and Amma shows her the sum shortcut.
story.extractedProblem: An AP starts at 100 and increases by 20 each month. What is the 12th term?

props.label: Equal jumps of 4: 3, 7, 11, 15… an AP with a = 3, d = 4

visual.caption: Every hop is the same size (d = 4). That equal step is what makes it an arithmetic progression.

teachingGallery.title: Spot the common difference
teachingGallery.note: Subtract any term from the next; if it's always the same, it's an AP.

examples.caption: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 — the 5 times table is an AP with d = 5.

props.label: n − 1 jumps of d reach the nth term

examples.caption: To reach the 5th term you take 4 jumps: a_5 = a + 4d.

standardMethod.summary: Find a and d, then use a_n = a + (n − 1)d for any term and S_n = n/2 × (2a + (n − 1)d) for the total.
steps: Write the first term a and the common difference d = second term − first term.
For the nth term, put values into a_n = a + (n − 1)d.
For the sum of n terms, use S_n = n/2 × (2a + (n − 1)d), or n/2 × (first + last) if you know the last term.
Check: does the difference stay the same across the whole list?

workedExamples.problem: Find the 10th term of the AP 3, 7, 11, 15, …
Find the sum of the first 10 terms of 3, 7, 11, 15, …

commonMistakes.mistake: Using a + n·d instead of a + (n − 1)d for the nth term.
commonMistakes.fix: To reach the nth term you take one FEWER jump than n: the 5th term needs only 4 jumps, so it is a + (n − 1)d.

commonMistakes.mistake: Taking d as positive when the list is going down (e.g. 20, 17, 14…).
commonMistakes.fix: d = next − previous. For 20, 17, 14 that is 17 − 20 = −3. A falling AP has a negative d.

easy.q: In the AP 2, 5, 8, 11, … what is the common difference d?
AP with a = 5 and d = 2. What is the 3rd term? (a + 2d)

medium.q: Sum of the first 5 terms of 4, 7, 10, … (a = 4, d = 3)

challenge.q: Sum of the first 100 whole numbers 1 + 2 + … + 100 (a = 1, d = 1)
Sunil finds the 6th term of a = 2, d = 3 as 2 + 6 × 3 = 20. What is the fix?
hintLadder: The 6th term needs how many jumps?

questions.q: 10th term of the AP with a = 1, d = 4?
Sum of the first 20 terms of 1, 3, 5, 7, … (a = 1, d = 2)?

hintLadder: An AP adds the same each time.

teachBackPrompt: Show a grown-up a savings plan that adds the same amount each month, and explain how a_n = a + (n − 1)d jumps to the 12th month without listing every month, and how S_n adds them all at once.

revisionCard.summary: AP = same jump d every time. nth term a_n = a + (n − 1)d (one fewer jump than n). Sum S_n = n/2 × (2a + (n − 1)d) = n/2 × (first + last). A falling AP has negative d.
